intro: The clearest split is that Elasticsearch uses a subscription model and Typesense uses a unknown model. Elasticsearch is Distributed full-text search and analytics engine built on Apache Lucene with a REST API; Typesense is Open-source typo-tolerant search engine with instant sub-50ms search and easy self-hosting. The data points Elasticsearch at e-commerce product search and documentation/knowledge base search, and Typesense at e-commerce product search and documentation/knowledge base search. Pricing: Elasticsearch is subscription from $95/mo (Standard Cloud); Typesense is unknown from $7/mo (cloud). Lock-in is low for Elasticsearch and low for Typesense, with DX at 5/5 vs 5/5 and transparency at 4/5 vs 3/5. On listed strengths, Elasticsearch emphasises e-commerce product search and documentation/knowledge base search, and Typesense emphasises e-commerce product search and documentation/knowledge base search. Both offer an enterprise tier for larger rollouts. Honest trade-off: neither fits simple projects, so if that is your context, look elsewhere; otherwise pick on the pivotal factor above and the DX score that matters most to your team.
quick_take: Elasticsearch is for e-commerce; Typesense is for e-commerce; decide based on which pricing model fits your budget.
verdict_a: Choose Elasticsearch if a subscription pricing model fits your procurement and your workload matches e-commerce. Skip it if your context is simple projects.
verdict_b: Choose Typesense if a unknown pricing model fits your procurement and your workload matches e-commerce. Skip it if your context is simple projects.

faq.a: Listed entry pricing is $95/mo (Standard Cloud) for Elasticsearch and $7/mo (cloud) for Typesense. Both offer a free tier for getting started. Total cost at scale depends on usage and plan details that are not in this dataset, so confirm with each vendor.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Elasticsearch to Typesense?
faq.a: Elasticsearch is tagged with low lock-in (low-medium — open-source (elastic license 2.0), but complex setup). Typesense is tagged with low lock-in (low — open-source, instantsearch-compatible adapter). Both sides are low lock-in, so a migration is feasible but still needs planning around data export and SDK rewrites.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, Elasticsearch or Typesense?
faq.a: Both are rated 5/5 for developer experience in this dataset. With equal scores, the choice comes down to the specific SDK surface and docs you will use day to day. Run a short spike on each before deciding.

faq.q: Is Typesense a good alternative to Elasticsearch?
faq.a: Yes for teams whose workload matches e-commerce, since both list the same fit. Typesense is flagged as not for simple projects, so confirm your context does not fall there.
